corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: As we see, for both additive and affine couplings, the invertible 1 × 1 convolution achieves a lower negative log likelihood and converges faster.

As we see in Table , our model achieves a significant improvement on all the datasets. (a) Smiling (b) Pale Skin (c) Blond Hair (d) Narrow Eyes (e) Young (f) Male Figure 6: Manipulation of attributes of a face.
Figure : We propose a generative flow where each step (left) consists of an actnorm step, followed by an invertible 1 × 1 convolution, followed by an affine transformation .
We compare the average negative log-likelihood (bits per dimension) on the CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) dataset, keeping all training conditions constant and averaging across three random seeds.

We choose the CelebA-HQ dataset , which consists of 30000 high resolution images from the CelebA dataset, and train the same architecture as above but now for images at a resolution of 256 2 , K = 32 and L = 6.
This flow is combined with a multi-scale architecture (right).
In this paper we propose Glow, a simple type of generative flow using an invertible 1 × 1 convolution.

Figure : Synthetic celebrities sampled from our model; see Section 3 for architecture and method, and Section 5 for more results. is to overcome these limitations by: (1) learning realistic world models, potentially allowing agents to plan in a world model before actual interaction with the world, and (2) learning meaningful features of the input while requiring little or no human supervision or labeling.
In this paper we work towards this ultimate vision, in addition to intermediate applications, by aiming to improve upon the state-of-the-art of generative models.
Since such joint models potentially capture all patterns that are present in the data, the applications of accurate generative models are near endless.
Autoregressive models, such as the Pixel-CNN (van den , are also reversible, however synthesis from such models is difficult to parallelize, and typically inefficient on parallel hardware.
The hidden layers of autoregressive models have unknown marginal distributions, making it much more difficult to perform valid manipulation of data.
In this paper we propose a new a generative flow coined Glow, with various new elements as described in Section 3.
In (MAF), the authors propose a generative flow based on IAF ; however, since synthesis from MAF is non-parallelizable and therefore inefficient, we omit it from comparisons.
We collect an i.i.d. dataset D, and choose a model p θ (x) with parameters θ.

We choose the architecture described in Section 3, and consider three variations for the permutation of the channel variables -a reversing operation as described in the RealNVP, a fixed random permutation, and our invertible 1 × 1 convolution.
Besides the permutation operation, the RealNVP architecture has other differences such as the spatial coupling layers.
In order to verify that our proposed architecture is overall competitive with the RealNVP architecture, we compare our models on various natural images datasets.
We also include the bits/dimension for our model trained on 256 × 256 CelebA HQ used in our qualitative experiments.

We choose the CelebA-HQ dataset , which consists of 30000 high resolution images from the CelebA dataset, and train the same architecture as above but now for images at a resolution of 256 2 , K = 32 and L = 6.
We propose a new type of flow, coined Glow, and demonstrate improved quantitative performance in terms of log-likelihood on standard image modeling benchmarks.
In addition, we demonstrate that when trained on high-resolution faces, our model is able to synthesize realistic images.
